file.content:
issues = linear.pending_issues(cycle_id)
    if not issues:
        print(f"Cycle {cycle_id} has no Todo/Backlog issues — nothing to do.")
        return 0

    for issue in issues:
        identifier = issue["identifier"]
        print(f"drain-cycle: picked {identifier}: {issue['title']}", file=sys.stderr)

        started_at = _now_iso()
        try:
            target_repo = repos.resolve(issue)
        except RepoResolutionError as exc:
            # Pre-spawn resolution halt: no Linear state was moved, so no
            # revert is attempted. The worktree path is the ``<unresolved>``
            # placeholder since no repo was chosen.
            state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
            halt_reason = (
                f"{_halt_message(identifier, state_name, Path(_UNRESOLVED_WORKTREE_DISPLAY))}"
                f" — {exc}"
            )
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=_now_iso(),
                exit_code=-1,
                final_linear_state=state_name,
                REDACTED,
                halt_reason=halt_reason,
            )
            print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
            return 1

        try:
            worktree_path = worktree.add(target_repo, identifier)
            # Orchestrator owns the Todo→In Progress half so the lifecycle
            # doesn't depend on the spawned agent's compliance. The agent
            # still owns the …→Done half via Linear MCP — see prompt.py tail.
            linear.set_state(issue["id"], _IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME)
        except Exception as exc:
            # Convert any pre-spawn failure into a recorded halt rather than
            # a traceback: write a run-log entry with the planned worktree
            # path, print the halt message, exit non-zero. Subsequent issues
            # are not attempted — same contract as a spawn-time halt.
            planned_path = target_repo / worktree.WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
            state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
            halt_reason = (
                f"{_halt_message(identifier, state_name, planned_path)}"
                f" — setup failed: {exc}"
            )
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=_now_iso(),
                exit_code=-1,
                final_linear_state=state_name,
                worktree_path=str(planned_path),
                halt_reason=halt_reason,
            )
            print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
            return 1

        agent_prompt = prompt.build(issue, worktree_path)
        worker_model = model.resolve(issue)

        result = worker.run_issue(
            claude_cmd=_CLAUDE_CMD,
            model=worker_model,
            prompt=agent_prompt,
            cwd=worktree_path,
            token_limit=limits.per_issue_tokens,
            time_limit_seconds=limits.per_issue_seconds,
            cost_limit_usd=limits.per_issue_cost_usd,
        )
        finished_at = _now_iso()

        if result.breach is not None:
            # The worker crossed a per-issue cap (tokens or time) and was
            # process-group killed (grandchildren reaped). Same revert + halt
            # contract as a not-Done exit, with the recorded usage of the
            # killed session and the breached cap named in the halt reason.
            original_state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
            effective_state, revert_error = _revert_to_pre_halt_state(
                issue["id"],
                target_state_name=original_state_name,
                pre_revert_state_name=_IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME,
            )
            halt_reason = (
                f"{_halt_message(identifier, effective_state, worktree_path)}"
                f" — {result.breach.describe()}"
            )
            if revert_error is not None:
                halt_reason += (
                    f"; revert to {original_state_name!r} failed: {revert_error}"
                )
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=finished_at,
                exit_code=result.exit_code,
                final_linear_state=effective_state,
                worktree_path=str(worktree_path),
                halt_reason=halt_reason,
                **_worker_log_fields(result),
            )
            print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
            return 1

        refreshed = linear.get_issue(issue["id"])
        post_spawn_state = refreshed["state"]["name"]
        is_done = refreshed["state"]["type"] == _DONE_STATE_TYPE

        if is_done:
            # Append unconditionally for every attempted issue.
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=finished_at,
                exit_code=result.exit_code,
                final_linear_state=post_spawn_state,
                worktree_path=str(worktree_path),
                halt_reason=None,
                **_worker_log_fields(result),
            )
            worktree.remove(target_repo, worktree_path)
            print(f"drain-cycle: {identifier} done; worktree removed.", file=sys.stderr)

            # Cycle-wide circuit breaker: every issue may stay under its own
            # per-issue caps while their sum drains the quota. Check the
            # running totals (which now include this Done issue) before

content.text:
## Code Review: ABA-237 token/time/cost guardrails

I reviewed the full working-tree diff (4 new files, 6 modified) across all five axes.

### Correctness ✓
- **Per-issue token breach** — `_monitor` polls `accumulator.cumulative()` under lock, kills the process group on `observed >= token_limit`. Verified by a real-subprocess test that reaps a grandchild (proves group kill, not just direct-child).
- **Per-issue time breach** — same loop, `elapsed >= time_limit_seconds`. The old 3600s timeout's safety role is preserved (kills a session emitting no usage).
- **Cycle breach** — checked after each Done issue against the run-log running totals; stops before the next spawn. Tested via the death-by-aggregate path (6M × 2 ≥ 10M cap, third issue never attempted).
- **`--max-budget-usd`** — present when cost cap set, absent when `None`, prompt stays trailing positional. Both tested.
- **`limits.yml`** — absent→defaults, empty→defaults, `null`→off, number→override; rejects unknown keys / non-positive / non-numeric / bool / invalid YAML. All tested.
- Edge: `yaml.safe_load` (not unsafe), bool rejected before the `int` check (since `bool ⊂ int`), `0`/negative rejected.

### Readability ✓
`limits.py` is cohesive and well-documented; `Breach.describe()` is the single wording source for both per-issue and cycle halts so the surfaces can't drift. Orchestrator cycle-check is clearly commented.

### Architecture ✓
Clean dependency direction: `worker → limits` (Breach), `orchestrator → limits` (Limits, check_cycle), `cli → limits` (load). No cycles. Mirrors the existing `repos.py` config pattern (error type, `default_config_path`, eager CLI validation). `breach: Breach | None` is a clean generalization of the old `timed_out: bool`. No dead code — `_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS` and all `timed_out` references fully removed.

### Security ✓
No secrets, no injection. `--max-budget-usd` value is formatted from an operator-controlled, validated positive number. `safe_load` prevents arbitrary object construction from `limits.yml`.

### Performance ✓
1s polling over a minutes-long session and per-Done cycle-total recomputation (≤15 entries) are both negligible.

### Findings
- **FYI:** The token cap is "kill if *still running* past the cap." An issue that crosses the cap on its final turn and exits cleanly (marking Done) completes rather than being retroactively halted — killing finished work would be pointless, and the cycle cap still accounts for those tokens. Intended behavior.
- **FYI:** With both per-issue token and time caps off, the worker blocks on `proc.wait()` with no outer timeout — the documented, explicit consequence of disabling both.

**Verdict: Approve.** No Critical or Required findings. 120 tests pass, no flakiness across 3 runs of the subprocess-based breach tests.

Proceeding to commit and push per the repo workflow.

aiTitle: Add token/cost guardrails and worker kill logic

content.text: Now commit with a conventional-commit message (repo style: ≤70 char subject, no Co-Authored-By trailer):

input.command:
git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
feat: guardrails to kill runaway workers and stop cycles (ABA-237)

Before this the only ceiling on a worker was a 3600s wall-clock timeout,
and the cycle had none — one issue could burn 43M tokens / 23 min and a
handful could drain the whole quota with no circuit-breaker.

drain_cycle/limits.py defines per-issue and cycle-wide caps on tokens,
wall-clock, and cost, enforced in two layers:

- Native belt: the per-issue cost cap is passed to claude as
  --max-budget-usd so the session self-terminates on spend.
- Orchestrator suspenders: the worker polls the live event stream and
  SIGKILLs the session's process group when its cumulative tokens or
  elapsed time cross the per-issue cap; the orchestrator sums the run
  log's running totals after each Done issue and stops the cycle when a
  cycle-wide cap is crossed (the death-by-aggregate case the per-issue
  caps can't see).

Token count is the primary guardrail (subscription user); cost rides
alongside. Defaults — per-issue 8M tok / 20 min / $15; cycle 30M tok /
90 min / $60 — are live with no config and overridable per cap via an
optional ~/.drain-cycle/limits.yml (absent=default, null=off,
number=override; malformed halts startup). Each guardrail is
independently on/off-able.

WorkerResult.timed_out becomes breach: Breach | None, naming the cap and
the value at kill time; the per-issue breach takes the existing exit-1 +
revert + halt_reason contract, and a cycle breach is recorded in the run
log's new top-level cycle_halt_reason. The hardcoded _ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS
is absorbed into the configurable per_issue_seconds cap.
EOF
git log --oneline -1
